methodology: Pass 1 (baseline): the agent answers all 100 apps from model knowledge alone, no tools. This is fast and cheap but stale for anything younger than the training cutoff, and blind to naming collisions.

Pass 2 (tool-verified): for a stratified sample of 9 apps (picked across categories, weighted toward newer/smaller/ambiguous products where a model is most likely to guess wrong), the agent re-runs with web_search + web_fetch against live docs and corrects itself.
Pass 3 (human spot-check): a human re-opens the same doc URLs the agent cited and confirms the specific claim (auth type, self-serve gate, MCP status) actually appears on that page.
4 fields were scored per sampled app: auth method, self-serve status, MCP existence, buildability verdict. 9 apps x 4 fields = 36 scored fields.

field_results.pass2: native - every Cloud workspace ships an MCP server

sample.note: Model knowledge undercounted MCP maturity for smaller/faster-moving open-source tools.

field_results.pass2: self-serve, but programmatic app-token provisioning needs a support request

sample.note: Cleanest verification in the sample - old, stable, well-documented API, first pass held up.

field_results.pass2: partner-gated - credentials only issued to an existing paid customer by a system-admin role
workaround - needs an existing paid tenant

sample.note: Biggest single miss in the sample: a fully public docs site (api.docs.dealcloud.com) reads exactly like a self-serve API on the surface. Only fetching the credential-provisioning page revealed the admin-gated reality. This is exactly the kind of docs-vs-reality gap the assignment is testing for.

field_results.pass1: unclear - name collides with an unrelated open-source GraphQL framework also called Pylon

field_results.pass2: native - Pylon MCP listed in their own docs

field_results.pass1: blocked - couldn't identify the right product

sample.note: A real failure mode, not a knowledge gap: the search agent's first query returned a same-named but unrelated product (getcronit/pylon, a GraphQL framework). A second, more specific query (usepylon.com) found the right one. Left uncaught, this would have shipped a wrong verdict for a real customer-requested app.

field_results.pass2: self-serve, key visible in dashboard immediately

sample.note: Small/newer vendor with essentially zero footprint in general training data. One targeted search (apidocs.fan) fully resolved it.

field_results.pass2: self-serve, but only bundled into paid plans - not available on Lite/Standard/free
ready, but gate is the price tier, not the auth

sample.note: Common miss pattern: assuming 'has an API key' means 'freely buildable.' The real gate here is commercial, not technical.

field_results.pass1: none - assumed no API exists at all
field_results.pass2: Enterprise tier now has a documented Google Cloud API; consumer product still has none
partner-gated (Enterprise API is Google Cloud/IAM-provisioned, not open signup)
community MCP wrappers exist (cookie-auth automation, not official)

sample.note: This one shows why 'search anyway even when confident' matters: the flat 'Google never shipped an API for this' belief was true for a long time and stopped being fully true - stale confidence is its own failure mode.

field_results.pass1: API key (default guess for a SaaS meeting tool)
field_results.pass2: OAuth2, via an official MCP server

field_results.pass1: none (assumed, since 'meeting notes' apps rarely ship MCP)

sample.note: Undersold by the first pass - this category (AI meeting tools) is moving fast enough that 2025-era assumptions about API maturity are already out of date.

field_results.pass2: API key, confirmed via cloud.higgsfield.ai and a Make.com integration listing

sample.note: Same pattern as fanbasis: small/new vendor, first pass had nothing to go on, one search resolved it fully.

scoreboard.note: pass2_accuracy is 100% by construction for this sample - these are the 9 apps we deep-verified against live docs, not a claim that the other 91 are equally certain. See confidence field on every row of the full dataset: 9 are 'verified' this way, 58 are 'high confidence' (large, stable, extremely well-documented platforms - Stripe, GitHub, Slack-tier), 25 are 'medium' (correct-shaped answer, not re-checked this pass), and 8 are 'low' or openly unresolved.

honestly_unresolved.issue: No public developer API or docs surfaced after searching; product markets integrations/webhooks but not a documented API. Flagged blocked rather than guessed.

Could not confirm which live product the brief's 'waterfall.io (contact/company intel)' hint refers to - searches returned unrelated same-named products. Flagged for direct human follow-up instead of fabricating an answer.

No distinct public developer portal found under this name; NMI-style gateways in general provision credentials only after merchant underwriting, so the verdict (partner-gated) is a category-level inference, not a confirmed one.
The docs URL given in the source brief did not resolve to verifiable public documentation in this pass.

No broadly documented self-serve public REST API found; access appears to route through an MCP server rather than an open API-key signup, but the auth model behind that MCP server wasn't independently confirmed.
The brief's own hint ('OAuth requested') suggests gated/requested access; no public API reference was found to confirm scope or terms.
API existence is documented but pricing-tier gating and exact auth flow are lower-confidence - not independently re-verified this pass.
